mistake: Skipped PREFLIGHT assessment for iteration 8 session. Went directly to work without epistemic baseline, resulting in 'No PREFLIGHT checkpoint found' warning. Cannot calculate learning deltas or calibration accuracy for this session.

prevention: Always start new sessions with PREFLIGHT assessment, even for 'quick' tasks. The 30 seconds to assess baseline saves confusion later and enables proper learning measurement. Could add CLI reminder: 'No PREFLIGHT detected - create one now? [y/n]'

mistake: .gitignore exposed internal project structure in public GitHub repo - revealed other platforms (twitter, discord, HN), planning doc patterns (*PLAN*.md, REDDIT_APPLICATION*.md), and internal framework details (.empirica/)
why_wrong: Misunderstood .gitignore purpose - thought it was for privacy control, but it's a PUBLIC tracked file visible to everyone on GitHub. .gitignore prevents accidental commits, NOT information disclosure
cost_estimate: 2 hours to detect + fix (nuclear repo rebuild). Could have leaked project scope to Reddit API reviewers
prevention: ALWAYS use generic .gitignore patterns. Never list specific internal folders/files that reveal project scope. Remember: Everything in git is public (files, history, messages, .gitignore). For true privacy, don't commit at all

mistake: CRM schema has inverted relationship: engagements link to goals instead of projects
why_wrong: Goals are already project-scoped. The chain client->engagement->goal->project is backwards. A client should link to projects directly (many-to-many), with engagements scoped to client-project pairs. Current design forces traversing through goals to find which projects a client is associated with.
prevention: When designing relational schemas, trace the natural hierarchy first: clients own projects, projects own goals. Link at the ownership level, not the leaf level.

mistake: Spent hours debugging Tailscale SSH ACL rules when the actual blocker was the ts-input iptables chain DROP rule. We assumed ACL changes would propagate to the local firewall for shared nodes.
why_wrong: Tailscale SSH ACL is a separate system from regular SSH over Tailscale. The ts-input iptables chain is the LOCAL enforcement layer and doesn't auto-populate per-peer ACCEPT rules for cross-tailnet shared nodes. We should have checked iptables first when tcpdump showed zero inbound packets.
prevention: When Tailscale connectivity fails: (1) FIRST check `sudo iptables -L ts-input -n` for DROP rules, (2) tcpdump showing zero packets = local filter problem not ACL problem, (3) Don't conflate Tailscale SSH (managed SSH) with regular SSH over Tailscale tunnel

We assumed ACL changes would propagate to the local firewall for shared nodes.", "why_wrong": "Tailscale SSH ACL is a separate system from regular SSH over Tailscale. The ts-input iptables chain is the LOCAL enforcement layer and doesn't auto-populate per-peer ACCEPT rules for cross-tailnet shared nodes. We should have checked iptables first when tcpdump showed zero inbound packets.", "cost_estimate": "3 hours", "root_cause_vector": "CONTEXT", "prevention": "When Tailscale connectivity fails: (1) FIRST check `sudo iptables -L ts-input -n` for DROP rules, (2) tcpdump showing zero packets = local filter problem not ACL problem, (3) Don't conflate Tailscale SSH (managed SSH) with regular SSH over Tailscale tunnel"}

mistake: Used Claude Code built-in Task agents for parallel investigation instead of Empirica's own epistemic agent system (agent-spawn/agent-parallel). The built-in agents don't track epistemic state, don't link to sessions, and don't produce structured noetic artifacts.
why_wrong: Empirica's agent system exists precisely for this use case — parallel investigation with epistemic tracking. Using generic agents bypasses the framework we're building and loses the meta-learning data. The results were useful but unstructured and not linked to the session's epistemic graph.
prevention: When spawning investigation agents, always use Empirica's agent-spawn or agent-parallel commands. Reserve Claude Code's Task tool for non-epistemic work (file operations, simple searches).
